deductions.evidence: In the top schematic, "K1" and "K2" are printed directly beneath a small contact-terminal box ("3"-O-"13") positioned next to the CR terminal strip (A1X A2X A3X A4X / A1Y A2Y A3Y A4Y / CR). In the bottom ladder diagram, "(K1)" and "(K2)" appear as parenthetical labels flanking the single "CR" coil circle itself (node -(K1)- CR -13- (K2)- O.L.), in the same small-parenthetical style used throughout the print for wire/terminal numbers (e.g. (A1Y)(A1X), (A2Y)(A2X)). No second or third coil/contactor symbol with its own device tag is drawn anywhere in the print -- the only relay/coil device tag present is "CR". K1/K2 read as the two wire-number designators for CR's own coil leads, not as a pair of separate contactor devices.
deductions.correction: State that K1 and K2 are wire/terminal numbers on the CR relay coil's two leads (matching the "3"/"13" pin box), not two separate contactor devices; note no second contactor coil symbol appears in the print.

deductions.evidence: The print shows a single fuse device labeled "FU" with "1A" printed immediately beside/below it (both in the top terminal-block diagram and at the head of the ladder diagram, "FU 1A"). "1A" reads as the fuse's 1-amp rating, not a separate "fuse holder" component.
deductions.correction: Identify "FU 1A" as a single fuse rated 1 amp, not a fuse plus a separate fuse-holder component.

deductions.claim: Motor (M) with a lamp or indicator (L.B.)
deductions.evidence: The actual 3-phase motor is drawn as a separate labeled box ("MOTOR" with leads T1, T2, T3) at the lower-left of the top diagram. The small circled "M" tagged "(L.B.)" sits instead inside a different dashed sub-assembly (with Mov., Rect., Econ. Cap., Res. components) that is not wired to the T1/T2/T3 motor leads, and no bulb/lamp symbol is drawn at that location -- the adjacent symbol reads as a switch/contact, not a lamp.
deductions.correction: Distinguish the 3-phase MOTOR (T1/T2/T3) from the small circled "M" near "(L.B.)", which belongs to the separate operating-mechanism sub-circuit; do not assert an unlabeled "lamp or indicator" without a visible bulb symbol.

deductions.claim: Omitted: Mov. (varistor), Rect. (rectifier bridge), and 1 Res./2 Res. (resistor blocks), all clearly labeled in the print
deductions.evidence: "Mov.", "Rect.", "1 Res.", and "2 Res." are all directly labeled in the top schematic and bottom ladder diagram (the operating-mechanism sub-circuit alongside the Econ. Caps the candidate does list), but none is mentioned anywhere in the response.
deductions.correction: List the Mov. (varistor), Rect. (rectifier), and 1 Res./2 Res. (resistor) components that are visibly labeled in the mechanism sub-circuit.

deductions.claim: it appears to energize the control relay (CR) which in turn energizes contactor K1 and K2, allowing power to the motor
deductions.evidence: Per the K1/K2-as-wire-numbers reading above, CR does not "energize" K1/K2 as downstream devices -- K1/K2 are the wire numbers feeding CR's own coil. The print's actual main-power-switching path is never described: L1/L2/L3 feed through the 1C.T./2C.T./3C.T. current transformers toward T1/T2/T3, and the visible operating-mechanism sub-circuit (Mov./Rect./Econ. Caps driving the circled "M") is the element CR's auxiliary contacts (A2X/A2Y, A3X/A3Y, A4X/A4Y) actually operate. This mechanism, which appears to be what actually closes the power path to the motor, is omitted entirely in favor of an invented "K1, K2 contactors" narrative.
deductions.correction: Describe that Start energizes the CR coil (via leads K1/K2); CR's auxiliary contacts then drive the motor-operated mechanism (M, through Rect./Mov./Econ. Caps) that performs the actual power switching, rather than describing "K1/K2 contactors" as the switching devices.

deductions.claim: Omitted: the CR holding/seal-in contact wired in parallel with the Start button
deductions.evidence: The ladder diagram shows a "CR (A1Y)(A1X)" contact symbol drawn in parallel with the Start pushbutton (the standard 3-wire seal-in arrangement) -- this is never mentioned in the described sequence, even though the response otherwise walks through the Start/Stop/CR sequence step by step.
deductions.correction: Describe the CR seal-in (holding) contact in parallel with Start: once CR picks up, this contact holds the coil energized after the Start button is released, until Stop or O.L. opens the circuit.

deductions.claim: energize the control relay (CR) which in turn energizes contactor K1 and K2
deductions.evidence: This reverses the actual coil/terminal relationship visible in both the top pin-box ("3"-O-"13" under "K1"/"K2") and the ladder diagram ((K1)-CR-(K2) directly flanking the single CR coil): K1/K2 are wire numbers on CR's own leads, so CR is not a separate stage that subsequently "energizes" K1/K2 as if they were downstream relay logic.
deductions.correction: Correct the causal chain: CR's coil itself is fed via leads K1/K2, not the other way around; do not present K1/K2 as a second logic stage energized by CR.

deductions.claim: Nothing major appears to be unreadable in the provided image. All labels and connections seem clear based on the provided OCR data.
deductions.evidence: No OCR text extraction was supplied to the candidate for this case (OCR was unavailable for this benchmark's cases per the judge protocol's OCR-grounding caveat) -- the claim of reliance on "provided OCR data" references a data source that did not exist for this run. The claim that all labels/connections "seem clear" is also contradicted by the response's own misreadings of the small parenthetical terminal labels (K1/K2) and its omission of several labeled components (Mov., Rect., Res.).
deductions.correction: State the readability assessment as based on visual inspection of the image alone, without referencing OCR data that was not supplied, and note which small labels (e.g. K1/K2, A1X-A4Y) carry lower confidence.

deductions.claim: The contactors (K1, K2) must close to allow power to the motor.
deductions.evidence: This restates the invented two-contactor device as an observed operating requirement, in a third separate section of the response (after the component list and the theory-of-operation section), with full confidence and no hedging -- an assumption about K1/K2's function stated as an established fact rather than flagged as uncertain given the print's contrary wire-number convention.
deductions.correction: Qualify the K1/K2 characterization as uncertain, or correctly identify them as CR coil wire numbers, instead of restating "contactors K1, K2" as fact across multiple sections.

deductions.evidence: Listed as a specific troubleshooting target in "What would stop it from working"; because K1/K2 do not correspond to a real, separate physical contactor (see component_label_recognition deduction above), directing a technician to inspect "contactor K1, K2" for faults points at a device that is not actually present in the described form, risking wasted troubleshooting time versus checking the CR relay and the motor-operated mechanism.
deductions.correction: Flag the K1/K2 mechanism explanation as tentative, or correct it, so a technician does not search for a nonexistent second/third contactor during troubleshooting.

notes: Print is a clean, legible line-art scan (Bulletin 509 Sizes 7/8, standard Start-Stop wiring) -- classification and most primary device tags (L1/L2/L3, CTs, CR, O.L.'s, FU, Econ Caps, Start/Stop, the T1/T2/T3 MOTOR) are correctly read. The dominant, repeated error is treating "K1"/"K2" as two separate contactor devices energized by CR, when both the top pin-box ("3"-O-"13" under K1/K2) and the ladder diagram ((K1)-CR-(K2) directly flanking the single CR coil) show K1/K2 in the same small-parenthetical wire-number style used elsewhere in the print (A1X/A1Y, etc.) -- i.e. they are wire numbers on CR's own coil leads, not a second relay stage. This invented two-contactor narrative also causes the response to omit the print's real operating mechanism (Mov./Rect./Econ. Caps driving a motor-operated "M" element via CR's auxiliary contacts) and the CR seal-in contact. Compounding this, section 6 claims reliance on "provided OCR data" that was not supplied for this case and asserts full readability despite the misread labels. overall_confidence is high because the K1/K2-as-wire-number reading is corroborated by two independent, consistent visual cues (the top pin-box and the ladder-diagram coil flanking) and by the print's own internal labeling convention.
